SUMMARY:
More importantly, rational design and controllable synthesis of catalysts have been pushed to a new stage along with the vigorous development in single-atom catalysis.7 Although the development of single-atom catalysts continued to flourish,8,9,10,11,12,13 the catalytic activity still remains to be improved, which may be severely restricted by the density of the isolated metal active centers14,15,16 and their intrinsic activity.17 In fact, arduous efforts have been dedicated to regulate the intrinsic activity, which is based on control and optimization of the energy level structure and surface electronic structure.18 Hence, it can improve the catalytic performance even with a limited number of active sites, which is different from increasing density by adding the number of accessible catalytic active sites.

Atomic-level regulation strategies of single-atom catalysts: Nonmetal heteroatom doping and polymetallic active site construction

The bigger picture
------------------

With the technological and scientific progress in synthesis, characterization, and surface science, as well as theoretical and computational chemistry, the development of catalysts has gradually transformed from selection done “blindly” through trial and error to research based on rational design. In recent years, single-atom catalysis and its atom-level regulation research have received enormous interest from academic research, bringing light to atomically precise catalysis. Remarkably, atom-level regulation of single-atom catalysts has a particular focus on the interatomic interfaces and interactions between active atoms, which are expected to offer a comprehensive and deep understanding of catalytic processes through exploration of the potential interatomic synergy effect. By virtue of single-atom catalysts and their atom-level regulation, rational design and controllable synthesis has been pushed to a new stage. In this review, we survey relevant progress in atomic-level regulation strategies, and we summarize the critical factors for effective regulation and improvement of single-atom catalysts.

Summary
-------

Single-atom catalysts have become the forefront and hotspot in catalysis research due to their unique structural characteristics such as extreme atomic utilization efficiency and uniformity of reaction centers. To break through the bottleneck on their intrinsic activity enhancement, arduous efforts have been made to regulate the metal single-atomic sites. Herein, with a special interest in interatomic interfaces and interactions, our review focuses on the atomic-level regulation strategies of single-atom catalysts, including nonmetal [heteroatom doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/heteroatom-doping "Learn more about heteroatom doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and polymetallic active site construction, referring to the introduction of nonmetal or metal atoms, respectively, into primitive metal single-atomic sites. We summarize the progress of these two strategies in optimizing single-atom active sites from the perspectives of synthesis, application, and mechanism. We start from a systematic overview of the atomic-level regulation methods and corresponding fabrication routes, followed by a comprehensive summary of the current advances in some typical [electrocatalytic reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalytic-reaction "Learn more about electrocatalytic reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Then we expound on the synergistic reaction mechanism and the intrinsic structure-activity relationship after regulation. In the final part, we discuss the challenges and opportunities of these two atomic-level regulation strategies for further development. Our review of the atomic-level regulation of single-atom catalysts gives significant insight into the rational design of atom-efficient catalysts and adds depth and breadth to the fundamental understanding of atomically precise catalysis by elucidating its intrinsic mechanisms.

Introduction
------------

Single-atom catalysts are a new catalytic material family composed of isolated metal single-atom and support material with distinctive structural properties,[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib1),[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib2) such as isolated metal active centers, extreme dispersion, highly unsaturated coordination environment, and uniform reaction centers.[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib4),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib5),[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib6) By virtue of their unique structural characteristics, single-atom catalysis has been regarded as a pioneer of atomically precise catalysis. More importantly, rational design and controllable synthesis of catalysts have been pushed to a new stage along with the vigorous development in single-atom catalysis.[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib7) Although the development of single-atom catalysts continued to flourish,[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib8),[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib10),[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib12),[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib13) the catalytic activity still remains to be improved, which may be severely restricted by the density of the isolated metal active centers[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib14),[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib15),[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib16) and their intrinsic activity.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) In fact, arduous efforts have been dedicated to regulate the intrinsic activity, which is based on control and optimization of the energy level structure and surface electronic structure.[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib18) Hence, it can improve the catalytic performance even with a limited number of active sites, which is different from increasing density by adding the number of accessible catalytic active sites.

In recent years, atomic-level regulation strategies for single-atom catalysts have received enormous interest from the academic research community. Inspired by the [heteroatom](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/heteroatom "Learn more about heteroatom from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") doping modification, a common strategy is to introduce nonmetal heteroatoms with different [electronegativity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electronegativity "Learn more about electronegativity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") from the atoms in the original support, such as N, S, P, O, B, etc.,[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib19),[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib20) called nonmetal [heteroatom doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/heteroatom-doping "Learn more about heteroatom doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") strategy. In fact, the active center of single-atom catalysts usually refers to the collective entity of the central metal single atom and the coordination atoms from the support, in which the coordination atoms exert unprecedented influence on the formation, stability, and overall catalytic performance of single-atom metal active centers,[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib3),[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib21) and become a more promising point for catalysts modification. Nonmetal heteroatom doping strategy introduces nonmetal heteroatoms so as to change the type or number of coordination atoms, which can achieve a significant change on the [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") state, electronic structure, and local spatial configuration of the central metal atom.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) Furthermore, doped nonmetal heteroatoms may participate in the process of adsorption for reaction molecules and assist the formation of reaction intermediates, so as to adjust and enhance the catalytic performance.

In addition, another atomic-level regulation strategy is called construction of polymetallic active sites, derived from the adjustment mechanism of metal alloy structure.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) It introduces other atomic metal site into the single-atom catalysts for catalytic performance enhancement. To some extent, construction of polymetallic active sites can be regarded as metal atom doping, and is expected to further improve the activity and selectivity by their unique multi-metal active site structure. Up to now, the dual-atom catalysts (DACs) constructed by introducing only another one atomic metal site are a typical representative among them.[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib22) By formation of a homonuclear or heteronuclear dual-atom metal site, it might be possible to modulate the catalytic performance through various interatomic interactions (such as bifunctional effects, electronic structure reconstruction, and geometric configuration regulation).[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib23)

Atomic-level regulation strategies, including nonmetal heteroatom doping and polymetallic active site construction, are of great importance not only for fundamental research but also for practical application. (1) Firstly, atomic-level regulation based on defined active centers from single-atom catalysts constructs the new active centers with more precise atomic numbers and configurations than other methods. Consequently, it becomes a viable way to provides ideal models for structure-property investigation, bringing light to atomically precise catalysis.[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib24) (2) Secondly, it has a particular focus on the interatomic interfaces and interactions between active atoms, which are expected to acquire a comprehensive and deep understanding of the catalytic processes by exploring the [potential interatomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/interatomic-potential "Learn more about potential interatomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") synergy effect. (3) Thirdly, it has been demonstrated to effectively enhance the catalytic activity and selectivity in many Herein, this review predominantly aims at elucidating the atomic-level regulation strategies of single-atom catalysts, by which we are referring to atomic modification on the primitive metal single-atomic site by introducing nonmetal or metal atoms, respectively (shown as [Figure 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#fig1)). More specifically, we start from a systematic overview on their fabrication routes and methods, and next summarize their current advances applied in some typical [electrocatalytic reactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalytic-reaction "Learn more about electrocatalytic reactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), such as ORR, OER, HER, CO 2 RR, and so on. Then, we expound the synergistic reaction mechanism and the intrinsic structure–activity relationship after regulation. At the end of this review, we present the challenges and opportunities of these two atomic-level regulation strategies. Along with ceaseless innovation and maturation of its technology, atomic-level regulation for single-atom catalysts would significantly facilitate the rational design of high-efficiency nanocatalysts, and sharpens our understanding of atomically precise catalysis by elucidating the interatomic synergy mechanisms.

Nonmetal heteroatom doping
--------------------------

Nonmetal [heteroatom](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/heteroatom "Learn more about heteroatom from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") doping strategy is a typical strategy in the atomic-level regulation for single-atom catalysts, especially for carbon-based single-atom catalysts. [Carbon material](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/carbon-material "Learn more about Carbon material from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a kind of environmental-friendly material with high performance,[70](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib70) for its specific features such as high specific [surface area](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/surface-area "Learn more about surface area from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), superb chemical and mechanical dependability, adjustable morphological and structural combinations, tunable porosity and surface properties, and good electrical and [thermal conductivity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermal-conductivity "Learn more about thermal conductivity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").[71](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib71) Hence, carbon-based material can achieve different functions by ingenious design and precision synthesis for [chemical structure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/structure-composition "Learn more about chemical structure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is commonly used as metal-free catalysts or as a support in single-atom catalysts, and widely applied and researched in the field of catalysis, energy, ecology, and so on.[71](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib71),[72](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib72),[73](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib73) However, with the primitive carbon material there is difficulty in providing strong metal-support interaction to anchor isolate metal atoms,[74](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib74) making unsuitable to be directly used as a support material for preparation of single-atom catalysts.[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib17) Defects or heteroatom sites act as anchor sites for isolated metal atoms through strong metal-support interaction. The construction of carbon defect sites to coordinate with metal atoms is a relatively complex process, while it is easy to implement to dope nonmetallic heteroatoms in carbon materials.

Compared with the primitive carbon material, [heteroatom doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/heteroatom-doping "Learn more about heteroatom doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can effectively regulate the performance of the material and provide more suitable anchoring sites for the isolated metal atom, and play a role in regulating the [oxidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/oxidation-reaction "Learn more about oxidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") state and electronic structure of the central metal atom. The N element has high similarity and compatibility with the C element, so it has been the most deeply studied doped element. [N doping](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/n-type-doping "Learn more about N doping from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") cannot only increase the conductivity of carbon materials, but also improve the activity of carbon-based materials.[75](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib75) Recently, many studies have been carried out on the structure of multiple heteroatoms. These works are mostly carried out on the basis of N atom doping, introducing a second kind of heteroatom, such as S,[76](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib76) P,[77](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib77) O,[78](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib78) B,[79](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib79) etc. By participating in central metal-support coordination, heteroatom doping is able to reduce the high surface energy of the isolated metal atom, which not only stabilizes the highly dispersed metal single-atom active sites but also modulates the activity. It can also be used as an "environment atom" to replace the original carbon atom in the skeleton, and there is a long-range interaction between the support and the metal single-atom active site, effectively improving the catalytic activity of the catalyst. For example, Xie’s group reported a strategy of sulfur doping (S doping) to achieve effective regulation of the [electrocatalytic activity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/electrocatalytic-activity "Learn more about electrocatalytic activity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the Fe–NC active site.[80](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2667109323000933#bib80) They have demonstrated atomic dispersion of Fe–N _x_ species on N and S codecorated hierarchical carbon layers and employed the material as a [bifunctional catalyst](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/bifunctional-catalyst "Learn more about bifunctional catalyst from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The improvement of the electrocatalytic performance of Fe–N–C active centers after S atom doping was attributed to the uneven distribution of charge caused by doping and the increase of Fe 3d electron conductivity by sulfur substitution.

### Abstract

People have been looking for an energy-efficient and sustainable method to produce future chemicals for decades. Heterogeneous single-atom catalysts (SACs) with atomic dispersion of robust, well-characterized active centers are highly desirable. In particular, correlated SACs with cooperative interaction between adjacent single atoms allow the switching of the single-site pathway to the dual or multisite pathway, thus promoting bimolecular or more complex reactions for the synthesis of fine chemicals. Herein, the structural uniqueness of correlated SACs, including the intermetal distance and electronic interaction in homo/heteronuclear metal sites is featured. Recent advances in the production methods of correlated SACs, showcasing the research status and challenges in traditional methods (such as pyrolysis, wet impregnation, and confined synthesis) for building a comprehensive multimetallic SAC library, are summarized. Emerging strategies such as process automation and continuous-flow synthesis are highlighted, minimizing the inconsistency in laboratory batch production and allowing high throughput screening and upscaling toward the next-stage chemical production by correlated SACs.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Continual improvement of synthesis methods, including wet impregnation and atomic layer deposition, enhances the control over single-atom dispersion and atomic-level interactions.
Investigating alternative substrates and heteroatom doping strategies can lead to the discovery of novel materials that boost catalytic performance and stability.
Implementing advanced fabrication routes and process automation aims to improve the stability of single-atom catalysts while enabling scalable production for industrial applications.
